subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 18! In this unit you will learn how to describe exactly where something is and exactly how it moves. Mathematicians do this using coordinates – pairs of numbers that act like an address for every point on a grid. Once you can read and write coordinates, you can plot points, complete shapes, slide shapes across a grid using translation, and flip them using reflection. These are the skills behind maps, video games, computer graphics, GPS navigation and the design of almost every building you have ever seen.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Read and write coordinates using (x, y) notation.
Plot points accurately in the first quadrant and in all four quadrants.
Describe the position of a point precisely, including with negative coordinates.
Find the distance between two points that lie on the same horizontal or vertical line.
Find a missing vertex of a rectangle or square from the other vertices.
Translate a shape a given number of units left/right and up/down, and describe a translation.
Reflect a point or shape across the x-axis and the y-axis.
Apply all of these skills to solve real-world map, game and design problems.

concepts.title: The Coordinate Grid
concepts.explanation: A coordinate grid is made from two number lines that cross at right angles. The horizontal line is called the x-axis. The vertical line is called the y-axis. The point where they cross is the origin, and its coordinates are always (0, 0). Everything on the grid is measured from the origin, so it is the starting point for all your counting. Coordinates are like an address for a point. Just as a home has a street and a house number, every point has an x-coordinate and a y-coordinate. We always write them in a…
concepts.example: For example, the point (3, 2) means: start at the origin, move 3 spaces right, then move 2 spaces up. That lands you on the exact spot we call (3, 2). The order really matters. The point (3, 2) is not the same as (2,…

concepts.title: Plotting and Reading Points
concepts.explanation: Plotting a point means marking it on the grid when you are given its coordinates. Reading a point means writing its coordinates when you can see it on the grid. These are opposite skills, and you need both. Let us take them one at a time, using a clear, careful method each time. Suppose you must plot the point (4, 3). Follow these steps every single time:
concepts.example: Start your finger at the origin (0, 0).

concepts.title: The Four Quadrants and Negative Coordinates
concepts.explanation: So far we have stayed in the top-right corner of the grid, where both numbers are positive. But the axes are really full number lines, stretching in both directions. When we extend them, the grid splits into four regions called quadrants. This is the big new idea of Year 5, so read this section slowly. On the x-axis, moving right of the origin gives positive numbers, and moving left gives negative numbers. On the y-axis, moving up gives positive numbers and moving down gives negative numbers. So a coordinate can…
concepts.example: The four quadrants are numbered in an anticlockwise direction, starting from the top-right:

concepts.title: Distance Between Points
concepts.explanation: When two points lie on the same horizontal line or the same vertical line, you can find the distance between them just by counting squares – no ruler needed. The trick is to look at which coordinate they share. If two points share the same y-coordinate, they lie on the same horizontal line, and the distance between them is the difference of their x-coordinates. For example, from (1, 3) to (5, 3) both points have y = 3, so the distance is 5 − 1 = 4 squares. If two points share the same x-coordinate, they lie on the…
concepts.example: We use the idea of difference – the larger value minus the smaller value – so the distance always comes out positive. This works even when negative coordinates are involved. From (−3, 4) to (2, 4) the y-values match, so…

concepts.title: Shapes on the Grid – Finding a Missing Vertex
concepts.explanation: Coordinates connect algebra and geometry, because you can describe a whole shape just by listing the coordinates of its corners, or vertices. A very common question gives you three corners of a rectangle and asks you to find the fourth. Suppose three vertices of a rectangle are (1, 3), (1, 2) and (4, 2). To find the fourth vertex, look for the patterns in the coordinates you already have:
concepts.example: Plot the three known points so you can see the shape forming.

concepts.title: Translation – Sliding Shapes
concepts.explanation: A translation slides a shape to a new position without turning it or flipping it. The shape keeps the same size and the same orientation – it just moves. Every point of the shape moves the same distance in the same direction, which is why the shape does not get stretched or twisted. We describe a translation with two movements: how far left or right, and how far up or down. For example, “3 squares right and 2 squares up.” To translate a point, you simply add or subtract from its coordinates. Moving right adds to…
concepts.example: Here is the rule written neatly. To translate the point (x, y) by “a” squares right and “b” squares up, the new point is (x + a, y + b). A move to the left or down simply uses a negative value. Let us see it in action:

methods.title: How to plot a point
methods.example: Plot the point (5, 2) and describe how you moved.
steps: Start at the origin (0, 0).
Move along the x-axis by the first number (right if positive, left if negative).
Move parallel to the y-axis by the second number (up if positive, down if negative).
Mark and label the point (x, y).

methods.title: How to find the distance on a line
methods.example: A point sits 6 squares to the right of the origin, directly on the horizontal axis. What are its coordinates?
steps: Check the two points share an x-value (vertical line) or a y-value (horizontal line).
Find the difference of the other coordinate: larger minus smaller.
That difference is the distance in squares. Count squares to check.

methods.title: How to find a missing vertex of a rectangle
methods.example: In which quadrant is the point (−4, −1)?
steps: Plot the three known corners.
Find which pairs share an x-value and which share a y-value.
The missing corner takes the x from one side and the y from the other.

methods.title: Distance on a line
steps: Solution: Both points share x = 2, so they are on the same vertical line.
The distance is the difference of the y-values: 9 − 5 = 4 squares.
Check the distance on a line result carefully.

methods.title: Distance with a negative
steps: Solution: Both share y = 4, so they are on the same horizontal line.
The distance is the difference of the x-values: 2 − (−3) = 2 + 3 = 5 squares.
Counting squares on the grid confirms 5.

methods.title: Missing vertex of a rectangle
methods.example: Three vertices of a rectangle are (2, 1), (2, 4) and (6, 4). Find the fourth vertex.
steps: Solution: (2, 1) and (2, 4) share x = 2 (a vertical side).
(2, 4) and (6, 4) share y = 4 (a horizontal side).
The missing corner must line up with x = 6 and y = 1, so it is (6, 1).

workedExamples.title: Plot a point
workedExamples.prompt: Plot the point (5, 2) and describe how you moved.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start at the origin (0, 0). Move 5 spaces right along the x-axis, then 2 spaces up. Mark the dot and label it (5, 2). Right first, up second – that is the rule.

workedExamples.title: Read a point on the axis
workedExamples.prompt: A point sits 6 squares to the right of the origin, directly on the horizontal axis. What are its coordinates?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: It is 6 right and 0 up, so it is (6, 0). Because it sits on the x-axis, the y-coordinate must be 0.

workedExamples.title: Name the quadrant
workedExamples.prompt: In which quadrant is the point (−4, −1)?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The x is negative (so left) and the y is negative (so down). Left and down is the bottom-left region, which is the third quadrant.

workedExamples.title: Distance on a line
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Both points share x = 2, so they are on the same vertical line. The distance is the difference of the y-values: 9 − 5 = 4 squares.

workedExamples.title: Distance with a negative
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Both share y = 4, so they are on the same horizontal line. The distance is the difference of the x-values: 2 − (−3) = 2 + 3 = 5 squares. Counting squares on the grid confirms 5.

workedExamples.title: Missing vertex of a rectangle
workedExamples.prompt: Three vertices of a rectangle are (2, 1), (2, 4) and (6, 4). Find the fourth vertex.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: (2, 1) and (2, 4) share x = 2 (a vertical side). (2, 4) and (6, 4) share y = 4 (a horizontal side). The missing corner must line up with x = 6 and y = 1, so it is (6, 1).

workedExamples.title: Translate a point
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add 3 to x and subtract 2 from y: (1 + 3, 2 − 2) = (4, 0).

workedExamples.title: Describe a translation
workedExamples.prompt: A vertex moves from (2, 1) to (5, 3). Describe the translation.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Horizontal change: 5 − 2 = 3, so 3 right. Vertical change: 3 − 1 = 2, so 2 up. The translation is 3 squares right and 2 squares up. Every other vertex must move the same way.

workedExamples.title: Translate a whole triangle
workedExamples.prompt: Translate the triangle with vertices (2, 3), (4, 3), (3, 5) by 2 right and 1 up.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Move each vertex the same way. (2, 3) → (4, 4); (4, 3) → (6, 4); (3, 5) → (5, 6). Join them in the same order. The new triangle is the same size and shape, just shifted.

workedExamples.title: Reflect a point across the y-axis
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Reflecting across the y-axis keeps y the same and changes the sign of x: (3, 2) → (−3, 2).

workedExamples.title: Reflect a point across the x-axis
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Reflecting across the x-axis keeps x the same and changes the sign of y: (3, 2) → (3, −2).

workedExamples.title: A coordinate puzzle
workedExamples.prompt: Start at (2, 2). Move 3 right, then 4 up, then 1 left, then 2 down. Where do you end up?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Track it step by step. (2, 2) → 3 right → (5, 2) → 4 up → (5, 6) → 1 left → (4, 6) → 2 down → (4, 4).

activities.title: Coordinate Treasure Hunt
activities.materials: squared paper, coloured pencils, a ruler.
steps: Draw a 10×10 coordinate grid and label both axes from 0 to 10.
Place these on your map using coordinates: treasure chest (7, 4), pirate ship (2, 8), desert island (9, 1), safe harbour (1, 3), secret cave (8, 9).
Draw a path from the pirate ship to the treasure chest and write the coordinates it passes.
Write five riddles using directions, such as: “I am 3 units east and 2 units north of the safe harbour. What am I?” Test them on a family member.
Keep your treasure map – you can add more locations as you learn.

activities.title: Coordinate Picture Art
activities.materials: squared paper, coloured pencils, a ruler.
steps: Plot these points in order and join them to draw a simple house: (2, 2) → (2, 5) → (4, 7) → (6, 5) → (6, 2) → (2, 2).
Add a door from (4, 2) to (4, 4), and windows at (3, 4) and (5, 4).
Now design your own picture using at least eight points.
Write the coordinate instructions so a friend can recreate your picture without seeing it.

activities.title: Translation Adventure
activities.materials: squared paper, a paper triangle you cut out, coloured pencils.
steps: Draw a triangle with vertices (2, 3), (4, 3) and (3, 5).
Translate it 3 right and 2 up; draw and label the new triangle in a second colour.
Translate the original 2 right and 3 down; draw it in a third colour.
Write the new coordinates for each translation and check that every vertex moved the same way.

activities.title: Mirror Line Investigation (Reflection)
activities.materials: squared paper, a ruler, a small mirror if you have one.
steps: Draw the x-axis and y-axis on a grid that includes negative numbers.
Plot a small shape in the first quadrant, for example a triangle at (2, 1), (4, 1), (3, 3).
Reflect it across the y-axis and write the new coordinates. What do you notice about the x-values?
Now reflect the original across the x-axis. What do you notice about the y-values? Write down the pattern you found.

activities.title: Coordinate Battleship (Family Game)
activities.materials: two 10×10 grids and pencils, for two players.
steps: Each player secretly marks five “ships” on their grid as sets of coordinates (ships are rows of 2, 3 or 4 squares).
Take turns calling out a coordinate, such as (4, 7).
Your opponent answers “hit” or “miss.” Keep track of your guesses on a spare grid.
The first player to find all of the opponent’s ships wins. Talk about how naming coordinates in the right order helps you aim.

activities.title: Design Your Own Village Map (Investigation)
activities.materials: squared paper, coloured pencils, a ruler.
steps: Draw a 12×12 grid to represent a village near Kismaayo.
Place at least eight locations with coordinates: masjid, well, school, market, and homes.
Write turn-by-turn directions from the school to the market using coordinates, for example: “Start at (3, 2), move 4 right to (7, 2), then 3 up to (7, 5).”
Calculate the straight-line distance between two locations that share a row or a column.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand coordinates best? Was plotting, translating or reflecting the trickiest for you? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Reading Coordinates Rule
rules.text: Coordinates are written (x, y): x first, then y. x = how far right (or left). y = how far up (or down). Along the corridor, then up the stairs.

rules.title: Four Quadrants Rule
rules.text: Positive x = right; negative x = left. Positive y = up; negative y = down. Quadrants run anticlockwise from the top-right: 1st (+,+), 2nd (-,+), 3rd (-,-), 4th (+,-).

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Translation and Reflection Rules Translate a right, b up: (x, y) becomes (x + a, y + b). Left or down uses a negative. Reflect across the y-axis: (x, y) becomes (-x, y). Reflect across the x-axis: (x, y) becomes (x, -y).

terms: A pair (x, y) that fixes a point
One of the four regions of the grid
A value left of or below the origin
A slide with no turn or flip
A flip over a mirror line
commonMistakes: x first, then y – right before up
y-axis changes x; x-axis changes y
Move every vertex the same way

questions.question: What does “Coordinates” mean?
options: A pair (x, y) that fixes a point
One of the four regions of the grid
questions.answer: A pair (x, y) that fixes a point

A value left of or below the origin

A value left of or below the origin

selfAssessment: I can read and write coordinates using (x, y) notation.
I can plot points accurately in the first quadrant and in all four quadrants.
I can describe the position of a point precisely, including with negative coordinates.
I can find the distance between two points that lie on the same horizontal or vertical line.
I can find a missing vertex of a rectangle or square from the other vertices.
I can translate a shape a given number of units left/right and up/down, and describe a translation.
I can reflect a point or shape across the x-axis and the y-axis.
I can apply all of these skills to solve real-world map, game and design problems.
